target.title: feat(telegram): opt-in interleaved progress lane
target.body:
## Summary

Add an opt-in Telegram renderer that projects reasoning text and structured runtime events into a single durable live message on the reasoning lane, matching CLI ergonomics without changing execution semantics.

**Gated on three conditions:** `toolProgressEnabled && interleavedProgress === true && hasReasoningLane` — inherits every existing group/DM/visibility gate.

## What it does

When `channels.telegram.streaming.preview.interleavedProgress` is enabled:
- Reasoning text streams into the interleaved message with a "Thinking" header
- Tool starts append a sanitized line (tool name only, never raw args by default)
- Item/plan/approval/command/patch events append title-only summary lines
- A rolling timer (20s cadence) shows elapsed time + a wall-clock stamp while running, so a stalled lane is visible at a glance; cleared on completion
- The model's reply is delivered once as the polished answer message — it is **not** mixed into the Thinking lane
- Long turns spill into continuation messages instead of freezing at Telegram's length cap
- Falls back to the existing `pushStreamToolProgress` lane when the flag is off

## Key design choices

- **Projection-only** — no changes to prompts, model routing, tool execution, or auth
- **No raw data** — tool lines show name only; `interleavedToolArgs` is a separate opt-in for sanitized args
- **No new event contract** — consumes existing `onReasoningStream`, `onToolStart`, `onItemEvent`, `onPlanUpdate`, `onApprovalEvent`, `onCommandOutput`, `onPatchSummary` callbacks unchanged
- **Reasoning + tools only; reply stays out** — the lane renders reasoning (`onReasoningStream`) and tool/event lines; the model's reply prose is **not** folded into it. Folding reply text in and stripping it back out at delivery proved unreliable — the model often drafts an answer, runs tools, then re-emits a revised one, leaving several answer-like copies of which only the last matches the polished final text. So the reply is delivered exactly once, as the answer. For the interactive backend the proxy restores real thinking on its own `onReasoningStream`, so reasoning is unaffected; a defensive final-answer strip remains for redacted-thinking backends that bridge assistant text into the reasoning lane. Tagged payloads (`<think>reason</think>answer`) keep only the in-tag reasoning, so answer prose after `</think>` never lands in the lane.
- **Single reasoning stream, delta-append** — appended as monotonic deltas (a cumulative snapshot contributes only its new suffix), so re-delivery never duplicates and the lane reads chronologically reasoning → tool → reasoning → …. The overlap fold is guarded: it folds a full-tail re-send (and full-increment duplicates) but never a short/mid-word coincidence, so a fresh fragment never loses its leading character.
- **Continuation on overflow** — as the durable message approaches Telegram's 4096-char cap the lane spills into a fresh continuation message (carrying recent context) instead of letting the draft stream stop editing and freeze the lane
- **Rate-limit-aware cadence** — the rolling "still running" timer ticks every 20s (not every few seconds) so N concurrently-streaming Thinking messages stay under Telegram's per-chat edit rate and don't draw 429s (a 429 drops the edit and stalls the lane); the line carries a wall-clock stamp so a genuine stall is obvious
- **No empty Thinking** — a turn that reaches final delivery with no reasoning/tool/status content (and no running timer) renders nothing, so the lane never emits a bare "Thinking" header
- **Plain-text transcript** — reasoning tag markers (`<think>` etc.) are unwrapped so the lane shows the model's prose, never raw tags

## Prerequisite

- **#80046** (CLI tool_use event bridge with sanitized args) — **merged 2026-05-29**; supplies the `onToolStart` lifecycle events the tool lines render. This branch should be rebased onto current `main` (which now contains it) before merge.

The renderer otherwise consumes only existing callbacks and is default-off, so it depends on no unmerged work. (On the standard `claude-cli` backend, CLI-*native* reasoning text additionally benefits from #85381's `thinking_delta`; without it the lane simply shows tool/event lines. The interactive backend restores thinking via its proxy, so reasoning appears regardless. Either way the default tool-progress lane is unaffected — this is not a hard dependency.)

## Changed files

- `extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.ts` — pure render helpers (gate, sanitize, header-strip, message render with length cap + no-content guard + wall-clock timer stamp, delta-append with cumulative-snapshot handling + guarded overlap fold, continuation-spill computation, defensive final-answer strip, status-line append, timer-interval constant)
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — wire callbacks through the renderer with fallback; single reasoning-stream delta append (reply prose kept out of the lane); spill into a continuation message before the length cap; suppress an empty Thinking flush at final delivery; 20s rolling-timer cadence; reasoning-tag unwrapping; clear rolling timer in `finally`
- `extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts` — `stripReasoningTagsForInterleaved` (extract only in-`<think>` reasoning, dropping answer prose after `</think>`, preserving code regions)
- `src/config/types.telegram.ts` + `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` — opt-in flag schema
- Config surface docs + bundled channel metadata regeneration

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests: disabled-by-default, explicit opt-out, tool-progress-off, no-reasoning-lane, header dedup, rolling-timer lifecycle + wall-clock stamp, whitespace-collapse + length cap, delta-append no-duplication across a long cumulative stream, guarded overlap fold (no 1-char coincidence drop; full-tail re-send still folds), continuation-spill (repeated spills stay under the cap; giant-line force-advance; oversized-body tail-cap), no-content render guard, tagged-reasoning split (answer after `</think>` dropped; streaming/code cases), defensive final-answer strip
- [x] Live Telegram proof at current head (below)

## Verification

Local checks at the current head:

```
pnpm tsgo:extensions                                                 # typecheck — clean
pnpm exec oxlint extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.ts \
                 extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts \
                 extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts   # lint — clean
npx vitest run extensions/telegram/src/interleaved-progress.test.ts \
               extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.test.ts   # all green
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Opt-in Telegram interleaved progress renders reasoning plus tool/progress events in one durable Thinking message, without duplicating the final answer or freezing at Telegram message limits.

Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw Telegram gateway against Telegram, default-off feature explicitly enabled, two concurrent Telegram threads in one chat.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rebuilt and ran the gateway from the PR head, enabled channels.telegram.streaming.preview.interleavedProgress, sent concurrent Telegram prompts that produced reasoning, tool progress, long Thinking output, and idle tool waits, then inspected Telegram messages and the gateway outbound Telegram send log.

Evidence after fix: Screenshot embedded below plus redacted outbound Telegram send-log metrics copied below: continuation msg=34783 -> msg=34786, final answer absent from every Thinking edit, zero bare Thinking sends, 20s timer edits at 04:20:15 -> 04:20:35 -> 04:20:55, and 99 outbound calls with 0 failures.

Observed result after fix: Thinking lane showed reasoning plus tool line, rolled into a continuation before the cap, sent the polished answer once as its own message, cleared empty Thinking output, and avoided Telegram 429s under the two-thread concurrent run.

What was not tested: No broad channel matrix or release package lane in this PR; focused local proof covers the Telegram renderer helpers, schema, lint, and extension typecheck.

## Live proof (current head)

Captured on the current head with the default-off feature enabled, over a **two-thread concurrent** session. The screenshot shows the visible lane; the rest is verified from the gateway's **outbound Telegram send log** — every `sendMessage` / `editMessageText` the bot issues — which shows message-level behavior a screenshot can't (message ids, edit cadence, final-state content, timing). Wire-level facts below are message-level metrics (ids, lengths, ok/timing) and contain no command/path/host content.

**Normal interleaving — reasoning + tool-use line in the Thinking lane, with the rolling timer:**

![interleaved lane](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/66da4d16b5a9467a82a65134976bc66aa956519c/proof-assets/interleaved-progress-telegram-2026-05-30-toolline.png)

**Wire-level verification (gateway send log, current head, one two-thread concurrent session):**

- **Long-message continuation** — `msg=34783` grew to the 4000-char cap and rolled into a fresh continuation `msg=34786` (carrying the next tool line) instead of freezing; clean seam, no garble.
- **No duplicate final answer** — on that long thinking-heavy turn the final answer was delivered once as its own message and is **absent from every Thinking edit** of `34783`/`34786`.
- **No empty Thinking** — **zero** bare-`Thinking`-header sends across the session.
- **20s rolling-timer cadence (rate-limit relief)** — while idling on a blocking tool, the "still running" line re-edited at **exactly 20s intervals** (04:20:15 → 04:20:35 → 04:20:55, body static at 3764 chars), keeping concurrent Thinking messages under Telegram's edit rate. The wall-clock stamp exposes any genuine stall.
- **No rate-limiting** — **99** outbound calls across the two concurrent threads, **0** failures (no `429`s, no oversized-preview stop) — the exact concurrency that previously drew 429s.

target.title: feat(embedded-runner): expose prep stage timings
target.body:
## Summary
- Expose embedded runner prep stage snapshots on `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult`.
- Include the same prep stage summary on the `agent_end` hook event for plugin observability.
- Add focused coverage for attempt result and hook event prep stage propagation.
- Document the new optional hook field. No `CHANGELOG.md` change is kept.

## Current upstream status
Latest `origin/main` already has May embedded-runner/subagent performance work and trace logging for startup stages. It still does not expose the attempt prep stage summary to `agent_end` hook observers. This PR is narrowed to that missing diagnostics surface so plugins can collect stage timing evidence without patching core locally.

## Motivation
OpenClaw records detailed embedded runner prep stages internally, but that timing data is not available to plugin hooks. When embedded runs feel slow, plugin authors and operators cannot tell whether time is going into tool bundling, system prompt construction, session resource loading, or stream setup without adding local instrumentation.

Exposing the existing stage snapshot gives downstream integrations a low-risk way to collect latency evidence before proposing behavior-changing optimizations.

## Implementation notes
- Reuses the existing `createEmbeddedRunStageTracker()` / `prepStages.snapshot()` path.
- Keeps all new fields optional.
- Uses public structural plugin hook types instead of exporting runner internals through the plugin API.
- Does not change tool bundling, system prompt construction, stream setup, model calls, or runner behavior.
- Documents `agent_end.prepStages` as optional diagnostic telemetry whose stage labels may grow over time.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Embedded runner prep stage timings are visible to a real non-bundled plugin's `agent_end` hook without changing the embedded run outcome.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local OpenClaw source checkout, current PR head `bd0d2a5e84bfe145cf6cbb107445d6e54abe6bd8`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `openclaw.json`, temporary local plugin loaded through `plugins.load.paths` with `hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`, `pnpm openclaw agent --local`, and the repo mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary plugin that writes its `agent_end` event's `prepStages` to JSONL, configured it through `plugins.load.paths`, started `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs`, then ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-78381-proof.S5j02x/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-78381-proof.S5j02x/state/openclaw.json OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-openclaw-78381-proof OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_LOG=/tmp/openclaw-78381-proof.S5j02x/agent-end-prepStages.jsonl pnpm openclaw agent --local --agent main --session-id prep-proof-session-2 --message "Reply exactly OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK" --thinking off --json`, followed by a Node assertion over the plugin JSONL output.

Evidence after fix: Copied live assertion output from the current-head CLI/plugin proof:

```json
{
  "head": "bd0d2a5e84bfe145cf6cbb107445d6e54abe6bd8",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK",
  "hookSuccess": true,
  "hookHasPrepStages": true,
  "hookTotalMs": 94,
  "hookStageNames": [
    "workspace-sandbox",
    "skills",
    "core-plugin-tools",
    "bootstrap-context",
    "bundle-tools",
    "system-prompt",
    "session-resource-loader",
    "agent-session",
    "stream-setup"
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: A standalone embedded `openclaw agent --local` run completed successfully against the mock OpenAI server, returned `OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, and the temporary plugin's real `agent_end` handler observed `event.prepStages` with the expected prep stages. The current head also verifies `runEmbeddedAgent` returns `result.meta.prepStages` from the attempt prep-stage summary.

What was not tested: A live paid external model/provider run was not run. The proof used OpenClaw's deterministic local OpenAI-compatible response server to avoid provider latency/cost while still exercising the real embedded runner, local CLI, plugin loader, and `agent_end` hook dispatch.

## Validation
- `node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check` passed: `OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256`.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.empty-error-retry.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts --reporter=dot` passed: 2 files, 64 tests.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD && git diff --check` passed.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- Risk: plugin authors might treat stage names as permanent.
  - Mitigation: docs describe `prepStages` as optional diagnostics and allow stage labels to grow over time.

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for image, video, and music generation tool registration.
- Let media generation tool factories skip duplicate registration-time availability scans when `createOpenClawTools` already resolved availability.
- Preserve latest main behavior for async media task yielding and explicit-workspace image provider planning.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`; original warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call, plus current-head regression verification after rebasing onto `origin/main` at `4dad7bd93b`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; after the latest rebase, ran the focused media-tool regression suite, core test typecheck, formatter check, `git diff --check`, and `pnpm build` on current head `e242dccc50`.

Evidence after fix:

```text
original warm prep probe after fix:
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes

current-head regression verification:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts --reporter=dot
4 test files passed, 178 tests passed

./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
exit 0

git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
exit 0

pnpm build
exit 0; phase timings total 140.7s, slowest tsdown 91.0s
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50. The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain guarded by tests.

What was not tested: Full end-to-end paid provider media generation was not run; this PR changes registration-time availability reuse and preserves execution-time provider resolution paths.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native embedded subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP or CLI-backed native paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Update protocol/docs/tests/prompt snapshots for the additive field. No `CHANGELOG.md` change is kept.

## Current upstream status
Latest `origin/main` already has broad May subagent/runtime work and existing embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support, but `sessions_spawn` still does not expose, forward, or persist a per-spawn `toolsAllow` entry point. This PR is narrowed to that missing public/control-plane plumbing.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native embedded subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the same policy is persisted on child sessions and inherited by later embedded child turns when no explicit override is passed.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local OpenClaw source checkout, current PR head `25243d99b2009a0c437874ed68dca24ad8c8614c`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `openclaw.json`, `pnpm openclaw agent --local`, and repo mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server with request logging.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Seeded a temp `sessions.json` child session at `agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof-25243d99b2` with `runtimeToolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`, started `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs` with `MOCK_REQUEST_LOG`, then ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof.NRgAFc/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof.NRgAFc/state/openclaw.json OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-openclaw-78441-proof pnpm openclaw agent --local --session-key agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof-25243d99b2 --message "Reply with exactly OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_78441" --json`, followed by a Node assertion over the mock request log.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the CLI run and request-log assertion:

```text
[agent/embedded] [trace:embedded-run] startup stages: runId=c43c486b-23d0-4e83-8fd1-c0dd12f67cba sessionId=proof-subagent-session phase=attempt-dispatch totalMs=5818 stages=workspace:1ms@1ms,runtime-plugins:5605ms@5606ms,hooks:6ms@5612ms,model-resolution:113ms@5725ms,auth:67ms@5792ms,context-engine:1ms@5793ms,attempt-workspace:1ms@5794ms,attempt-prompt:0ms@5794ms,attempt-runtime-plan:24ms@5818ms,attempt-dispatch:0ms@5818ms
```

```json
{
  "head": "25243d99b2009a0c437874ed68dca24ad8c8614c",
  "sessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:tools-policy-proof-25243d99b2",
  "responsesCalls": 1,
  "inheritedToolNames": ["exec", "read"],
  "markerObserved": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: The later CLI turn succeeded, reached the mock `/v1/responses` endpoint exactly once, returned `OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_78441`, and the provider request exposed only the inherited `exec` and `read` tools from the persisted child-session `runtimeToolsAllow` field.

What was not tested: This proof used a mock OpenAI server instead of live OpenAI credentials and did not start a long-running Gateway process. Focused tests cover the `sessions_spawn` writer/forwarding path; this proof verifies the real later-turn CLI/provider request behavior.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] Prompt snapshot check
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`, `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*`
- Scenario the tests lock in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported runtimes, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, and appears in model-facing dynamic tool snapshots.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native embedded subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. Unsupported ACP and CLI-backed native paths reject the option clearly.

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native embedded subagent runs. Unsupported paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced.

## Repro + Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/commands/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts --reporter=dot` passed: 5 files, 159 tests, 3 Vitest shards.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check ...changed files...` passed.
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check` passed: `Prompt snapshots are current (7 files)`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts --reporter=dot src/agents/code-mode.test.ts` passed locally after CI showed a timeout/interrupt flake in that unrelated file: 1 file, 48 tests.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD && git diff --check` passed.
- `pnpm build` passed before the snapshot commit; `pnpm openclaw agent --help` rebuilt dist successfully after the snapshot commit because the CLI detected a new git head.
- Current-head CLI/mock OpenAI proof above passed.

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: schema exposure, native forwarding path, Gateway forwarding path, session persistence, embedded command runner propagation, persisted child-session allowlist inheritance, strict validation tests, ACP rejection tests, CLI-backed native fail-closed behavior for non-empty and empty allowlists, embedded empty-allowlist behavior, prompt snapshot update, formatter/typecheck/build/whitespace checks.
- Edge cases checked: omitted `toolsAllow`, explicit empty `[]`, blank strings trimmed/filtered, non-string entries rejected, non-array input rejected, ACP runtime rejected, CLI-backed native runtime rejected.
- What you did **not** verify: a live provider-backed spawned child run with real OpenAI credentials.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect CLI-backed native runs.
  - Mitigation: CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch because they cannot enforce the embedded-runner allowlist.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses the existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

target.title: fix: honor plugin auth markers from manifests
target.body:
## Summary

- Honor non-secret auth markers supplied by enabled external plugin manifests.
- Keep marker discovery process-stable, with lifecycle cache clearing on plugin metadata/index writes.
- Exclude disabled installed plugin roots from fallback manifest scanning so disabled plugins cannot continue to own marker values.
- Use this branch only as a temporary upstream review vehicle; the durable target is upstream `openclaw/openclaw`.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-auth-markers.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-metadata-scan.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --reporter=verbose src/agents/model-auth-markers.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-metadata-scan.test.ts`
- `env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 CI=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false corepack pnpm check:changed`

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: model/auth marker handling now respects enabled installed external plugin manifest metadata, ignores disabled installed plugin marker metadata, and refreshes marker lookup only through the plugin metadata lifecycle path.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS at PR head `bdc8191cdbd7926f68fc918563a7eeb8cee7a6e0`. The proof used repository production modules for persisted installed-plugin index writes, manifest metadata scanning, lifecycle cache clearing, and model auth marker lookup. No `.dev.vars`, live credentials, or private plugin config were read.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx -` with a redacted temp-home script that created enabled and disabled plugin manifests under `/private/tmp/openclaw-pr-88913-proof-*/home/.openclaw/extensions`, wrote the persisted installed-plugin index with `writePersistedInstalledPluginIndexSync(...)`, then called `listOpenClawPluginManifestMetadata()`, `isNonSecretApiKeyMarker(...)`, and the lifecycle-cleared marker path against `OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp>/home` and `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1`.

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the current-head temp-home proof:

```text
proof: temp home=<tmp>/home
proof: enabled manifest discovered=true
proof: disabled manifest discovered=false
proof: enabled marker accepted=true
proof: disabled marker accepted=false
proof: v2 marker before lifecycle clear=false
proof: v2 marker after index lifecycle clear=true
proof: v1 marker after index lifecycle clear=false
```

Observed result after fix: the enabled installed plugin manifest was discovered and its marker was accepted; the disabled installed plugin manifest was filtered and its marker was rejected; marker lookup stayed process-stable until the installed-index write cleared plugin metadata lifecycle caches, after which the new marker was accepted and the old marker was no longer accepted.

What was not tested: no networked marketplace/plugin registry install and no live third-party credentials were used; the proof exercises the installed-plugin index and manifest metadata production path with redacted temp files.

target.title: [Bug]: browser endpoint blocked by policy with remote CDP profile (browserless/chrome) — intermittent and inconsistent
target.body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

## Environment
- OpenClaw: 2026.5.28
- OS: Linux (Debian, Docker container)
- Browser container: browserless/chrome:latest
- Channel: Telegram
- Browser routing: OpenClaw Gateway → internal browser plugin → ws://browserless:3000 (Docker network) → browserless/chrome:latest → Chrome CDP

## Config
```json
"browser": {
  "enabled": true,
  "cdpUrl": "ws://browserless:3000?token=***",
  "attachOnly": true,
  "defaultProfile": "browserless",
  "remoteCdpTimeoutMs": 5000,
  "remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs": 8000,
  "profiles": {
    "browserless": {
      "cdpUrl": "ws://browserless:3000?token=***",
      "color": "#00AA00"
    }
  }
}
```

## Behavior
The browser tool and CLI both fail with `browser endpoint blocked by policy` when using a remote CDP profile.

Browserless logs show Chrome starts successfully and CDP WebSocket connects, but OpenClaw closes the session immediately (~300-700ms) without sending any navigation commands:

browserless:job: Inbound WebSocket request
browserless:job: Starting session
browserless:job: Proxying request to /devtools/browser route
browserless:job: Browser not needed, closing   ← closes without any Page.navigate

Gateway log:
[browser/service] Browser control service ready (profiles=3)
[tools] browser failed: browser endpoint blocked by policy raw_params={"action":"open","url":"https://example.com"}/

## Additional observations
- `openclaw browser status` shows `profile: browserless`, `running: true`, `cdpUrl: ws://browserless:3000/...` ✓
- Manual curl to Browserless CDP works: `curl http://browserless:3000/json/version?token=***` returns correct response ✓
- `openclaw sandbox explain` shows `browser` in allow list ✓
- `tools.alsoAllow: ["browser"]` set globally and per agent ✓
- CLI workaround (`openclaw browser --profile browserless open <url>`) works intermittently but also fails with same error after multiple calls
- Behavior is inconsistent: same command succeeds on one URL, fails on another 5 minutes later without any config change

## Expected behavior
`browser open <url>` should navigate to the URL using the remote CDP profile without being blocked by internal policy.

## Suspected cause
The internal browser control service HTTP endpoint (gateway.port + 2) does not bind when using remote CDP / attachOnly mode. The policy check happens against this local endpoint which is unreachable, causing all browser actions to fail regardless of CDP connectivity.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Set up browserless/chrome:latest as a separate Docker container in the same network as OpenClaw
2. Configure OpenClaw with a remote CDP profile pointing to `ws://browserless:3000?token=***`
3. Set `attachOnly: true`, `defaultProfile: "browserless"`
4. Add `browser` to `tools.alsoAllow` globally and per agent
5. Verify CDP is reachable: `curl http://browserless:3000/json/version?token=***` → returns valid response
6. Verify `openclaw browser status` shows `running: true`, `profile: browserless`
7. Ask the agent to open a URL or run: `openclaw browser --profile browserless open https://example.com`

### Expected behavior

OpenClaw connects to the remote Browserless CDP endpoint, navigates to the given URL, and executes browser actions (screenshot, snapshot, etc.) successfully.

### Actual behavior

The command fails with:
GatewayClientRequestError: browser endpoint blocked by policy

Browserless logs show Chrome starts and the CDP WebSocket connects, but OpenClaw closes the session immediately (~300-700ms) without sending any navigation commands:
browserless:job: Inbound WebSocket request
browserless:job: Starting session
browserless:job: Proxying request to /devtools/browser route: ws://127.0.0.1:XXXXX/devtools/browser/...
browserless:job: Browser not needed, closing   ← no Page.navigate ever sent

Gateway log:
[browser/service] Browser control service ready (profiles=3)
[tools] browser failed: browser endpoint blocked by policy raw_params={"action":"open","url":"https://example.com"}/

The behavior is intermittent: the CLI workaround (`openclaw browser --profile browserless open <url>`) occasionally succeeds but fails again after a few calls without any config change.

## Additional Observations
- `openclaw sandbox explain` shows `browser` in allow list ✓
- `tools.alsoAllow: ["browser"]` set globally and per agent ✓
- Manual CDP connectivity confirmed working ✓
- Internal browser control port (gateway.port + 2) does not bind in remote CDP / attachOnly mode — suspected root cause: policy check targets this unreachable local endpoint

## Suspected Cause
The internal browser control service HTTP endpoint (gateway.port + 2) does not bind when using `attachOnly: true` with a remote CDP profile. The policy check runs against this local endpoint, which is unreachable, causing all browser actions to fail regardless of actual CDP connectivity.

### OpenClaw version

 v2026.5.28

### Operating system

Debian 12

### Install method

_No response_

### Model

deepseek/deepseek-chat, anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5, google/gemini-2.5-flash - Same behavior across all models

### Provider / routing chain

OpenClaw (Docker) → OpenRouter → respective model provider

### Additional provider/model setup details

_No response_

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell

```

### Impact and severity

_No response_

### Additional information

_No response_

target.title: perf(memory): batch memory embeddings across files
target.body:
## Summary

- Source-wide memory indexing now groups dirty files by provider batch request capacity instead of the old 4096 chunk guard.
- OpenAI and OpenAI-compatible batch polling now backs off from the configured interval, reports provider `request_counts` progress when available, and preserves day-long timeout configuration.
- Regression coverage now locks in split oversized-file chunks staying in one source-wide provider batch, OpenAI-compatible polling progress/backoff, and 1440-minute batch timeouts.
- Current-head type coverage also accepts per-agent subagent thinking defaults used by the existing ACP runtime test.

Fixes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/88009

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw memory index --force --verbose` should batch a large mixed memory+session source set across file boundaries, including more than 4096 split chunks, instead of flushing early with `reason=max-chunks`; pending OpenAI batch status polling should back off and show provider progress instead of staying fixed at 2000ms with no progress counts.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux local OpenClaw CLI from `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-88238-20260601`, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, 833 memory markdown files, 495 session transcript files, provider `openai`, model `mistral/mistral-embed`, batch enabled, local OpenAI-compatible batch HTTP endpoint on `127.0.0.1:18184`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-88238-20260601
EVIDENCE_DIR=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-88238-evidence PORT=18184 \
  node /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-88238-evidence/prepare-maintainer-fixture.mjs
PORT=18184 STATS_PATH=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-88238-evidence/openai-compatible-batch-stats-maintainer.json \
  node /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-88238-evidence/openai-compatible-batch-server.mjs \
  > /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-88238-evidence/batch-server-maintainer.log 2>&1 &
server_pid=$!
OPENCLAW_HOME=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-88238-evidence/runtime-maintainer-home \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-88238-evidence/runtime-maintainer-home/.openclaw/openclaw.json \
NO_COLOR=1 \
pnpm openclaw memory index --force --verbose \
  > /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-88238-evidence/openclaw-memory-index-maintainer.log 2>&1
kill "$server_pid"
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
[memory] embeddings: source-wide batch prepare files=1328 sources=memory=833,sessions=495 maxFiles=2048 maxRequests=50000
[memory] embeddings: source-wide batch submit group=1 source=memory+sessions files=1328 chunks=10228 sources=memory=833,sessions=495 reason=end
[memory] openai batch batch-1 in_progress; waiting 2000ms
[memory] openai batch batch-1 in_progress; progress 0/10228 failed=0; waiting 4000ms
[memory] openai batch batch-1 in_progress; progress 3409/10228 failed=0; waiting 8000ms
Memory index updated (main).
```

```json
{
  "uploads": [
    {
      "fileId": "file-1",
      "requests": 10228,
      "firstInputs": [
        "# Log\nMemory file 0 source-wide batch marker.\n",
        "# Log\nMemory file 1 source-wide batch marker.\n",
        "# Log\nMemory file 2 source-wide batch marker.\n"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "batches": [
    {
      "batchId": "batch-1",
      "inputFileId": "file-1",
      "outputFileId": "output-batch-1",
      "requests": 10228,
      "endpoint": "/v1/embeddings",
      "completionWindow": "24h"
    }
  ],
  "statusPolls": [
    { "batchId": "batch-1", "poll": 1 },
    { "batchId": "batch-1", "poll": 2 },
    { "batchId": "batch-1", "poll": 3 }
  ],
  "outputReads": [
    {
      "outputFileId": "output-batch-1",
      "responses": 10228
    }
  ]
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The maintainer-sized mixed source set produced one source-wide OpenAI batch upload with 10,228 embedding requests across all 1,328 files, and the source-wide submit reason was `end`, not the old chunk-limit split. The wait loop also backed off from 2000ms to 4000ms and 8000ms while surfacing provider request progress from `request_counts`.
- **What was not tested:** A live third-party embedding provider was not tested because this environment has no external embedding credentials; the real OpenClaw CLI and OpenAI-compatible batch HTTP contract were tested locally at the network boundary.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: real CLI behavior proof, focused runtime tests, source-wide memory integration test, lint, and type lanes.
- Target test file: `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`; `src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts`; `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts`; `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/generic-embedding-provider.bridge.test.ts`; `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-policy.test.ts`; `extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts`; `extensions/openai/embedding-batch.test.ts`; `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/batch-http.test.ts`; `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/post-json.test.ts`.
- Scenario locked in: source-wide batching keeps chunks split out of oversized files in the same provider batch job, forced memory+session indexing shares one source-wide batch, OpenAI-compatible polling reports progress and backs off while pending, 1440-minute timeout config is preserved, and the existing OpenAI batch adapter still sends document input type requests.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it covers the real source queue, provider runtime bridge, OpenAI/OpenAI-compatible batch transports, and the exact large mixed-source behavior the reporter re-tested.

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts --reporter=verbose
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 23.72s

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts --reporter=verbose
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 69.44s

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/batch-runner.test.ts extensions/openai/embedding-batch.test.ts --reporter=verbose
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 33.25s

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/batch-http.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/post-json.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/generic-embedding-provider.bridge.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-policy.test.ts extensions/openai/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts extensions/openai/embedding-batch.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts --reporter=verbose
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 103.45s

pnpm check:test-types
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
all completed with exit code 0

node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
node scripts/run-bundled-extension-oxlint.mjs
both completed with exit code 0

git diff --check
exit code 0
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The source-wide memory batching layer reused a 4096 chunk guard as the source-level flush limit, so large mixed-source runs could split before the provider batch layer reached its 50,000 request capacity. The wait logs also ignored provider batch `request_counts`, so slow providers could look stalled even when they reported progress.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered cross-file batching and provider batch submission, but they did not cover files that split into many chunks while still remaining below the provider batch request limit, visible polling progress/backoff for pending remote batch jobs, or preservation of day-long batch timeout config.

target.title: fix: support Azure Responses text stream events
target.body:
## Summary

Companion/follow-up to #88893 for #88833. This branch addresses the code/test blockers called out on #88893 while preserving the same Azure Responses compatibility goal.

Changes:
- accept Azure Responses `text` content parts alongside OpenAI `output_text` in the shared parser;
- handle Azure `response.text.delta` in both parser paths;
- update `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` so the active Azure Responses transport path does not drop the same Azure stream shape;
- keep the SDK client/request seam typed to SDK `ResponseStreamEvent` and widen only the parser input to a narrow Azure-compatible event union;
- make streaming tests feed one continuous event sequence through `processResponsesStream`; and
- add coverage for Azure text deltas emitted without a preceding `response.content_part.added` event.

## Validation

Run locally on Windows with pnpm 11.2.2:

```text
npm exec --yes pnpm@11.2.2 -- test -- src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts
npm exec --yes pnpm@11.2.2 -- tsgo:core
npm exec --yes pnpm@11.2.2 -- tsgo:test:src
```

Focused test result: `src/llm/providers/openai-responses-shared.test.ts` 10 tests passed; `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts` 228 tests passed.

## Proof status

Draft on purpose: this does **not** add the missing redacted live Azure/OpenClaw canary proof requested on #88893. It only supplies the source and regression-test side of the review findings. Happy to close this if #88893 is updated/cherry-picked instead.

## Related

- #88893
- Fixes #88833 if completed with live proof/maintainer acceptance.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, the Telegram bot exhibited text "churn" on **ACP-dispatched turns** — users would briefly see a text message appear, then it would be deleted and replaced by a voice note with the same text as caption. This happened because `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` settled visible text **before** `maybeApplyTtsToPayload()`, so block text was always flushed to the channel before audio synthesis even started.

This PR eliminates the ACP-path churn by introducing a `captionedFinalText` channel capability and suppressing live ACP block delivery when captioned final TTS will be used.

**Scope**: This PR fixes the ACP dispatch path only, which is the primary production path for Telegram TTS. The non-ACP generic dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`) has a similar text-before-voice pattern but is pre-existing behavior and out of scope for this PR — it should be addressed in a follow-up issue.

## Root cause

```
Before (main):
  1. ACP projector delivers block text to user immediately
  2. TTS synthesis runs (2-5 seconds)
  3. Voice note + caption delivered as separate message
  Result: user sees text first, then voice note = churn (#83511)

After (this PR):
  1. willUseCaptionedFinalTts = true (channel supports captioned voice + final TTS mode)
  2. suppressBlockUserDelivery = true → NO live block text delivered to user
  3. TTS synthesis runs, text accumulated silently
  4. Voice note + caption delivered as single message (or text fallback if TTS fails)
  Result: user sees only the voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` gate: suppress ACP block delivery and defer text settlement for captioned final TTS; exclude `tagged` auto-mode from suppression (tagged mode is directive-conditional, unknowable at block time) |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` | Delivery coordinator: `suppressBlockUserDelivery` gate returns false for block delivery; accumulates text for fallback |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 3 regression tests: deferred settlement, block suppression + fallback, tagged-mode streaming preserved, tagged+directive TTS supplement |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.ts` | `captionedFinalText: true` capability declaration |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts` | Test for capability declaration |
| `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` | JSDoc for `captionedFinalText` capability contract |
| `src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts` | Capability seam test |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updated SDK API baseline hash |

## Tagged auto-TTS mode fix

When `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"`, TTS only fires for replies containing `[[tts:...]]` directives. The suppression gate now correctly excludes `tagged` mode from `willUseCaptionedFinalTts`, preserving live block streaming for ordinary replies. Tagged replies with directives still get TTS audio delivered as a supplement with `visibleTextAlreadyDelivered: true`.

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ 61 passed (61)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts
 ✓ 11 passed (11)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts
 ✓ 1 passed (1)

Total: 73 tests passed across 3 test suites
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram TTS final-mode "delete-and-resend churn" on ACP-dispatched turns — when TTS mode is `final`, users saw a text message appear, then a separate voice note arrived afterward. Root cause: ACP `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` settled visible text before TTS synthesis.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at commit `e33c474a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Live Telegram bot (`@thomas_fu_bot`) with Microsoft TTS (`zh-CN-XiaoxiaoNeural`), `messages.tts.mode: "final"`, `messages.tts.auto: "always"`, `channels.telegram.streaming.mode: "block"`.

- **Failing proof before fix (live Telegram)**:

  ![Before fix — separate text message + voice message (churn)](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/48AAB56AD0D1DC685CB3918F75AEB402)

  User sent "hello" to the Telegram bot. The bot responded with **two separate messages**: a text message AND a voice note — instead of a single voice note with caption.

- **Exact steps after this patch**:
  1. Applied patch via `npm link`
  2. Restarted Gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Sent messages from Telegram to the bot
  4. Observed: voice note with caption appeared directly — no text flash

- **Evidence after fix (live Telegram screenshot)**:

  ![After fix — voice messages with caption, no text churn](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/6937B66CC8A27C33B76FBD54155B542C)

  Voice notes delivered as single message with text caption. No separate text messages created.

- **What was not tested**: Non-ACP dispatch path (out of scope, pre-existing behavior). Other TTS providers (only Microsoft TTS tested live). Group chat TTS (tested in DM only).

## Out of scope / Follow-up

The non-ACP generic dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`) also has a text-before-voice pattern for final-mode TTS: blocks stream live, then TTS audio arrives as a separate supplement. This is pre-existing behavior unrelated to the regression reported in #83511 (which specifically describes ACP `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` ordering). A follow-up issue should be filed to address non-ACP TTS churn if desired.

Closes #83511

target.title: fix #85871: [Bug]: Heartbeat scheduler silently fails to fire on 5.20 and all 5.x versions (regression from 4.23)
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: scheduled heartbeat turns could reach the reply path while the same agent had active embedded work, where heartbeat turns are intentionally dropped.
- Solution: defer scheduled heartbeat dispatch earlier in `runHeartbeatOnce` when any active embedded run belongs to the target agent.
- What changed: heartbeat runner now checks active embedded-run session keys alongside active reply runs and returns the retryable `requests-in-flight` result before calling `getReplyFromConfig`.
- What did NOT change: manual/immediate heartbeat wake semantics, reply queue policy, outbound delivery, and heartbeat cadence calculation are unchanged.
- Fixes #85871

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Scheduled heartbeat dispatch now treats active embedded agent work as `requests-in-flight` instead of silently completing through the later drop path.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw task worktree rebased onto current `origin/main`, plus dev gateway startup in an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.scheduler.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-wake.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue-policy.test.ts`; `OPENCLAW_REPO=$TASK_WORKTREE OPENCLAW_LOG=$EVIDENCE_DIR/gateway-start-pr-88970.log OPENCLAW_TEMP_HOME=$EVIDENCE_DIR/gateway-home-pr-88970 OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$EVIDENCE_DIR/gateway-home-pr-88970/.openclaw-dev OPENCLAW_DEV_PID_FILE=$EVIDENCE_DIR/gateway-pr-88970.pid OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=19075 OPENCLAW_READY_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=120 ~/.claude/skills/daily-fix/scripts/start_openclaw_dev_gateway.sh`.
- Evidence after fix: The dev gateway runtime log excerpt from this patched worktree shows `[gateway] http server listening`, `[gateway] ready`, and `[heartbeat] started`. The focused regression command also shows the runner returned `{ status: "skipped", reason: "requests-in-flight" }` before model dispatch when active embedded work was present for the resolved heartbeat session and another same-agent session.
- Observed result after fix: In the live local OpenClaw gateway startup, heartbeat runtime initialized successfully; in the patched runner path, same-agent embedded work now produces the retryable busy result instead of entering the reply/model path that drops heartbeat turns.
- What was not tested: Waiting for a real external model heartbeat interval against a live user channel.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target test file: `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts` covers active embedded work on the resolved heartbeat session and another same-agent session. Existing scheduler/wake, outbound target, gateway runtime-service, and reply queue tests were also run.
- Scenario locked in: scheduled heartbeat dispatch returns `requests-in-flight` before model dispatch when active embedded work exists for the same agent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it tests the heartbeat runner boundary where the bug occurs, without relying on the downstream reply queue drop behavior or an external model/channel.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `runHeartbeatOnce` checked active reply runs and queued session lanes before calling `getReplyFromConfig`, but it did not check active embedded-agent runs for the same agent. When a scheduled heartbeat reached the reply layer while an embedded main-session run was active, the reply queue policy dropped heartbeat turns to avoid queueing heartbeat followups. That dropped reply surfaced to the heartbeat runner as an empty result, so the tick could be recorded as handled without ever delivering the heartbeat prompt to the main agent session.
- Why this is root-cause fix: the patch moves the deferral to the heartbeat runner boundary, before reply dispatch. It reuses the active embedded-run session-key registry and applies the same same-agent background-work invariant already used for active reply runs. That preserves the scheduler/wake retry contract: scheduled heartbeats now produce retryable `requests-in-flight` while embedded work is active, instead of relying on the downstream reply queue to handle a heartbeat it intentionally drops.
- Fix classification: Root cause fix
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High for the local active embedded-run deferral boundary; medium for the reporter's full deployed environment because I did not wait for a real external model heartbeat interval against a live user channel.
- Patch quality notes: This intentionally changes retry behavior at the dispatch boundary. The retry uses the existing heartbeat wake path for `requests-in-flight`; it avoids queueing heartbeat followups and avoids marking due heartbeat work as completed before a model turn actually runs.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: This changes the source dispatch decision before feedback/model execution. It does not alter mirrored/projection/persistence data or outbound delivery formatting.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.scheduler.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-wake.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts` — passed, gateway 2 files / 26 tests and infra 4 files / 150 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue-policy.test.ts` — passed, 2 files / 62 tests.
- Dev gateway startup from this task worktree — ready; heartbeat started.

target.title: Fix Discord gzip response parsing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord `message read` and `search` could receive raw gzip bytes and surface compressed binary instead of parsed JSON.
- Why it matters: callers expect parsed Discord API data; compressed bytes break message read/search workflows before normalization.
- What changed: the Discord REST client now defensively decodes raw gzip response bodies, and message read/search add response-shape guards with clear errors.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no Discord endpoint behavior, auth, request params, or network call shape changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature

target.title: fix(plugin-sdk): expose skills helper surfaces
target.body:
Fixes #81913

## Summary
- re-export workspace skill loaders and frontmatter metadata helpers from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/skills-runtime`
- re-export host env override sanitizer helpers from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/sandbox`
- add focused SDK contract tests for the new public helper surface

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: third-party plugins can import OpenClaw's supported skills registry, SKILL.md frontmatter, and system-run env sanitizer helpers through public plugin SDK subpaths instead of deep-importing internals or reimplementing them.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw package build from PR head `76d0d5f1c1` on macOS. I built the dist entrypoints, generated plugin SDK declarations, ran runtime postbuild shims, then imported the package through the public self-reference specifiers `openclaw/plugin-sdk/skills-runtime` and `openclaw/plugin-sdk/sandbox`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.plugin-sdk.dts.json --declaration true
node --experimental-strip-types scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts
node scripts/runtime-postbuild.mjs
node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-exports.mjs
node --input-type=module -e 'const skills = await import("openclaw/plugin-sdk/skills-runtime"); const sandbox = await import("openclaw/plugin-sdk/sandbox"); const frontmatter = skills.parseFrontmatter("---\nname: Demo\nmetadata: '\''{\"openclaw\":{\"skillKey\":\"demo-key\"}}'\''\n---\n"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ skillsRuntime: { hasLoadWorkspaceSkillEntries: typeof skills.loadWorkspaceSkillEntries === "function", hasParseFrontmatter: typeof skills.parseFrontmatter === "function", skillKey: skills.resolveOpenClawMetadata(frontmatter)?.skillKey }, sandbox: { dangerousHome: sandbox.isDangerousHostEnvOverrideVarName("HOME"), sanitized: sandbox.sanitizeSystemRunEnvOverrides({ shellWrapper: true, overrides: { LANG: "en_US.UTF-8", PATH: "/tmp/bin", TERM: "xterm-256color" } }) } }, null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
OK: All 4 required plugin-sdk exports verified.
```

```json
{
  "skillsRuntime": {
    "hasLoadWorkspaceSkillEntries": true,
    "hasParseFrontmatter": true,
    "skillKey": "demo-key"
  },
  "sandbox": {
    "dangerousHome": true,
    "sanitized": {
      "LANG": "en_US.UTF-8",
      "TERM": "xterm-256color"

}
```

Observed result after fix: the built package self-reference imports resolve from the public SDK subpaths, the new skills helpers are callable, the frontmatter metadata resolver returns the expected `skillKey`, and the sandbox env sanitizer filters `PATH` while preserving allowed shell-wrapper variables.

What was not tested: I did not install the package into a separate third-party plugin repo; the proof uses the built local package self-reference and the plugin SDK export checker.

target.title: fix(ui): pass agentId to sessions.list in Control UI session picker
target.body:
## Summary

- Control UI session picker called `sessions.list` without the selected agent's `agentId` parameter
- Backend `sessions.list` supports `agentId` filtering, but the Control UI never passed it
- Added `agentsSelectedId` to `SessionsState` type and pass it in the `loadSessions` request params

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI/UX (Control UI session picker)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Control UI session picker now passes the currently selected `agentId` to `sessions.list`, so the picker only shows sessions belonging to the selected agent.

**Environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, pnpm test.

**Steps run after the patch:** Ran Control UI tests in `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` and `ui/src/ui/app-settings.test.ts`.

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
pnpm test --run ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.test.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
```

**Observed result:** All UI tests pass. The `sessions.list` RPC now receives `agentId` when an agent is selected.

**Not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI with real gateway session filtering.

## Root Cause

`loadSessions` in `ui/src/ui/controllers/sessions.ts` constructed the `sessions.list` request params with only `includeGlobal`, `includeUnknown`, `activeMinutes`, and `limit` — never including `agentId`. The `SessionsState` type also lacked an `agentsSelectedId` field.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a specific agent is selected in Control UI, the session picker will only display sessions belonging to that agent, matching the backend's `agentId` filter behavior.

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

Closes #86183

target.title: fix(compaction): bound stale transcript usage
target.body:
Fixes #81178

## Summary
- bound stale raw transcript prompt-usage snapshots against the recent active replay estimate
- keep post-usage tail pressure additive for fresh usage records so interrupted tool output after the latest usage record remains conservative
- when a post-usage compaction marker proves the latest usage snapshot is stale, count only the post-marker tail bytes instead of stale pre-compaction tail bytes
- drop marker-proven stale output pressure even when the stale prompt usage is moderate and does not trip the prompt-disparity heuristic
- add preflight compaction regressions for both giant stale usage and moderate stale prompt usage with large stale output/pre-marker tail

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: stale pre-compaction transcript usage, stale pre-marker tail bytes, and stale output tokens should not force another preflight compaction when a post-usage compaction marker proves that the active post-compaction replay is small and no explicit transcript-byte policy is exceeded.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source runtime from PR head `b90c31584d` on macOS, using a real temporary JSONL session transcript and the actual `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` path. The compact dependency was instrumented only to count whether the runtime attempted compaction.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: from the PR checkout at `b90c31584d`, ran a `node --import tsx` source-runtime proof that wrote a transcript with stale assistant usage `input=9000`, stale assistant usage `output=80000`, a 450000-byte stale pre-marker tool result, a compaction marker, and a small post-compaction replay; then it invoked `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` with a 100000-token context window.

Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the direct source-runtime proof:

```json
{
  "head": "b90c31584d",
  "behavior": "post-usage compaction marker drops stale output and pre-marker tail pressure",
  "staleUsageInputTokens": 9000,
  "staleOutputTokens": 80000,
  "preMarkerTailBytes": 450000,
  "contextWindowTokens": 100000,
  "compactCalls": 0,
  "phaseCalls": [],
  "returnedOriginalEntry": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: the source-runtime preflight call returned the original session entry, never entered the `preflight_compacting` phase, and made zero compaction calls despite stale output plus stale pre-marker tail pressure that would otherwise exceed the configured 100k context window.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Discord/Pi provider session because the failure is in the local preflight estimator and the source-runtime proof exercises that estimator path directly.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts` (25 passed)
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `git diff --cached --check`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main`
- `node --import tsx -e ...` source-runtime proof copied above

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable hook-owned cache key.
- Keep the cache conservative: the key includes provider hook identity plus provider/model/runtime inputs and ordered original tool schema signatures; cached values clone only tool `parameters`, not tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, SDK docs/API surface for `resolveToolSchemaCacheKey`, and focused cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, eviction, and runtime-handle coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, execute closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current head `d892395657`, rebased onto latest `origin/main` at `4dad7bd93b`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rebuilt the branch from current `origin/main`, regenerated the plugin SDK API baseline, ran the focused schema/runtime tests, core test typecheck, formatter and diff checks, and `pnpm build`.

Evidence after fix:

```text
node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts --reporter=dot
3 Vitest shards passed: 1 file / 11 tests, 3 files / 234 tests, 1 file / 55 tests

exit 0

./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 docs/plugins/architecture-internals.md docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/plugins/provider-hook-runtime.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/types.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check origin/main...HEAD && git diff --check
exit 0

pnpm build
exit 0; phase timings total 132.0s, slowest tsdown 83.9s
```

Observed result after fix: the provider schema-normalization cache stores normalized parameters once and reuses cloned parameters on subsequent matching calls while preserving fresh tool closures. Regression coverage also proves cache keys are resolved from the same prepared provider runtime handle used for normalization, preventing cross-plugin reuse.

What was not tested: full live-provider end-to-end latency was not tested. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, the Telegram bot exhibited text "churn" on **ACP-dispatched turns** — users would briefly see a text message appear, then it would be deleted and replaced by a voice note with the same text as caption. This happened because `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` settled visible text **before** `maybeApplyTtsToPayload()`, so block text was always flushed to the channel before audio synthesis even started.

This PR eliminates the ACP-path churn by introducing a `captionedFinalText` channel capability and suppressing live ACP block delivery when captioned final TTS will be used.

**Scope**: This PR fixes the ACP dispatch path only, which is the primary production path for Telegram TTS. The non-ACP generic dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`) has a similar text-before-voice pattern but is pre-existing behavior and out of scope for this PR — it should be addressed in a follow-up issue.

## Root cause

```
Before (main):
  1. ACP projector delivers block text to user immediately
  2. TTS synthesis runs (2-5 seconds)
  3. Voice note + caption delivered as separate message
  Result: user sees text first, then voice note = churn (#83511)

After (this PR):
  1. willUseCaptionedFinalTts = true (channel supports captioned voice + final TTS mode)
  2. suppressBlockUserDelivery = true → NO live block text delivered to user
  3. TTS synthesis runs, text accumulated silently
  4. Voice note + caption delivered as single message (or text fallback if TTS fails)
  Result: user sees only the voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` gate: suppress ACP block delivery and defer text settlement for captioned final TTS; exclude `tagged` auto-mode from suppression (tagged mode is directive-conditional, unknowable at block time) |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` | Delivery coordinator: `suppressBlockUserDelivery` gate returns false for block delivery; accumulates text for fallback |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 3 regression tests: deferred settlement, block suppression + fallback, tagged-mode streaming preserved, tagged+directive TTS supplement |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.ts` | `captionedFinalText: true` capability declaration |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts` | Test for capability declaration |
| `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` | JSDoc for `captionedFinalText` capability contract |
| `src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts` | Capability seam test |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updated SDK API baseline hash |

## Tagged auto-TTS mode fix

When `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"`, TTS only fires for replies containing `[[tts:...]]` directives. The suppression gate now correctly excludes `tagged` mode from `willUseCaptionedFinalTts`, preserving live block streaming for ordinary replies. Tagged replies with directives still get TTS audio delivered as a supplement with `visibleTextAlreadyDelivered: true`.

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ 61 passed (61)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts
 ✓ 11 passed (11)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts
 ✓ 1 passed (1)

Total: 73 tests passed across 3 test suites
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram TTS final-mode "delete-and-resend churn" on ACP-dispatched turns — when TTS mode is `final`, users saw a text message appear, then a separate voice note arrived afterward. Root cause: ACP `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` settled visible text before TTS synthesis.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at commit `e33c474a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Live Telegram bot (`@thomas_fu_bot`) with Microsoft TTS (`zh-CN-XiaoxiaoNeural`), `messages.tts.mode: "final"`, `messages.tts.auto: "always"`, `channels.telegram.streaming.mode: "block"`.

- **Failing proof before fix (live Telegram)**:

  ![Before fix — separate text message + voice message (churn)](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/48AAB56AD0D1DC685CB3918F75AEB402)

  User sent "hello" to the Telegram bot. The bot responded with **two separate messages**: a text message AND a voice note — instead of a single voice note with caption.

- **Exact steps after this patch**:
  1. Applied patch via `npm link`
  2. Restarted Gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Sent messages from Telegram to the bot
  4. Observed: voice note with caption appeared directly — no text flash

- **Evidence after fix (live Telegram screenshot)**:

  ![After fix — voice messages with caption, no text churn](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/6937B66CC8A27C33B76FBD54155B542C)

  Voice notes delivered as single message with text caption. No separate text messages created.

- **What was not tested**: Non-ACP dispatch path (out of scope, pre-existing behavior). Other TTS providers (only Microsoft TTS tested live). Group chat TTS (tested in DM only).

## Out of scope / Follow-up

The non-ACP generic dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`) also has a text-before-voice pattern for final-mode TTS: blocks stream live, then TTS audio arrives as a separate supplement. This is pre-existing behavior unrelated to the regression reported in #83511 (which specifically describes ACP `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` ordering). A follow-up issue should be filed to address non-ACP TTS churn if desired.

Closes #83511

target.title: fix(sessions): honor explicit default model pins
target.body:
## Summary
- Persist explicit user selections of the configured default model as exact session model pins instead of clearing them as if they were unpinned defaults.
- Keep `/model default`, `session_status(model=default)`, and `sessions.patch model:null` as the explicit clear/unpin paths.
- Carry the exact selection through reply, queued-run, agent-command, reset, status, and sessions.patch paths so channel/parent/config fallback policy cannot silently replace user intent.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Explicit `/model <configured default>` selections no longer lose user intent just because they match the configured primary; fallback policy treats them as strict user session selections, while `/model default` still clears back to unpinned behavior.
Real environment tested: Local beatrice.local source checkout at PR head `98ca4cd8b150ee0be7fcd602a01c4aed346f316c` on May 30, 2026.
